perturbation.original_text: to use all reasonable endeavours to effect and maintain adequate security measures to safeguard such Confidential Information from unauthorised access, use and misappropriation;
perturbation.changed_text: to use what it considers reasonable efforts to effect and maintain security measures to safeguard such Confidential Information from unauthorised access, use and misappropriation;
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'what it considers reasonable efforts' weakens the obligation to maintain adequate security measures, allowing the recipient to subjectively determine the level of security, which can be lower than industry standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of GDPR mandates implementing appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, considering the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons. The modified text introduces a subjective standard ('what it considers reasonable') which can contradict the GDPR's objective of ensuring a risk-based, appropriate level of security.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires "all reasonable endeavours" for security measures, while the modified text only requires "what it considers reasonable efforts." The Law Explanation states that Article 32 of GDPR mandates "appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk." The Perturbation Explanation indicates that the change allows the recipient to subjectively determine the level of security, which can be lower than industry standards. This introduces an ambiguity, allowing the recipient to potentially implement security measures that do not meet the GDPR's standard of "appropriate to the risk", creating a legal contradiction under the Ambiguities category.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the completion or termination of the Business Purpose, each recipient shall promptly deliver up to the disclosing party all Materials supplied by the disclosing party incorporating any Confidential Information of that party and all copies thereof and destroy or erase any Confidential Information contained in any materials and documentation prepared by or on behalf of the recipient or recorded in any memory device. Within fourteen ሺ14ሻ days of such request or completion of the Business Purpose the recipient shall certify in writing to the disclosing party that it has fully complied with its obligation s under this Clause.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the completion or termination of the Business Purpose, each recipient shall endeavor to deliver up to the disclosing party all Materials supplied by the disclosing party incorporating any Confidential Information of that party and all copies thereof and destroy or erase any Confidential Information contained in any materials and documentation prepared by or on behalf of the recipient or recorded in any memory device if feasible. Within a reasonable time after such request or completion of the Business Purpose, the recipient shall certify to the disclosing party that it has substantially complied with its obligations under this Clause.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly deliver up' to 'shall endeavor to deliver up', adding 'if feasible' regarding destruction of information, and changing 'fully complied' to 'substantially complied' weakens the obligation to return and destroy confidential information, introducing ambiguity about the extent and timing of compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018, which implements the EU Trade Secrets Directive in the UK, provides legal remedies against the unlawful acquisition, use or disclosure of trade secrets. The original clause ensures that the disclosing party regains control over its confidential information at the end of the agreement. By weakening the obligation to promptly return and fully destroy such information, the modification creates an opportunity for the recipient to retain or improperly use the information, potentially leading to a violation of trade secret rights and contravening the intent of the regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the original obligation to return and destroy confidential information, introducing ambiguity about the extent and timing of compliance. By changing 'shall promptly deliver up' to 'shall endeavor to deliver up' and adding 'if feasible' regarding destruction of information, the recipient's obligation is lessened. This contradicts the intention of the Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018, as explained by the LLM, because it creates an opportunity for the recipient to retain or improperly use the information. The regulation aims to provide legal remedies against the unlawful acquisition, use or disclosure of trade secrets. The first snippet, referring to "The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018," supports the relevance of this law. The modifications introduce an ambiguous legal obligation, making it difficult to enforce the original intentions, as defined in the Perturbation Definitions, and therefore falls under 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The signatories agree to keep confidential the identity of any kind and all contacts so provided by any other signatories. Such identity including names, addresses, telephones, telefax n umbers, telex numbers or any ot her means, is the property of the introducing signatory and shall not be disclosed otherthan under the terms thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The signatories agree to respect the confidentiality of the identity of any kind and all contacts so provided by any other signatories. Such identity including names, addresses, telephones, telefax n umbers, telex numbers or any ot her means, is considered sensitive information and should generally not be disclosed except when necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agree to keep confidential' with 'agree to respect the confidentiality' and 'shall not be disclosed' with 'should generally not be disclosed except when necessary' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. The term 'respect' is less binding than 'keep confidential' and the addition of 'except when necessary' creates a loophole for disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Data Protection Act 2018, which supplements the GDPR in the UK, requires strict adherence to data protection principles, including purpose limitation (data should only be processed for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes). By weakening the obligation to keep contact details confidential and introducing the vague exception 'except when necessary,' the modified clause allows for potential disclosure of personal data without a clear legal basis, potentially violating the data protection principles outlined in Section 170 of the Data Protection Act 2018 and the GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: UK GDPR guidance and resources | ICO Skip to main content UK GDPR guidance and resources Due to the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025, this guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The Plans for new and updated guidance page will tell you about which guidance will be updated and when this will happen. Back to top
perturbation.justification: The change from 'agree to keep confidential' to 'agree to respect the confidentiality' and 'shall not be disclosed' to 'should generally not be disclosed except when necessary' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. According to the Law Explanation, this violates data protection principles outlined in Section 170 of the Data Protection Act 2018 and the GDPR. The original text provided a clear directive against disclosure, while the changed text introduces an exception that undermines the confidentiality obligation, potentially conflicting with the data protection principles requiring explicit and legitimate purposes for processing. The scraped snippet references the UK GDPR, indicating the relevance of data protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall make or permit others to make any reference to the subject matter of the Agreement, or the Confidential Information or use the name of the other party in any public announ cements, promotional, marketing o r s a l e s materials or efforts without the prior written consent of the o ther party and such consent shall not be unrea sonably withheld or delayed.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party should make any reference to the subject matter of the Agreement, or the Confidential Information or use the name of the other party in any public announcements, promotional, marketing o r s a l e s materials or efforts without first attempting to obtain the prior written consent of the o ther party, and such consent will not be unreasonably withheld if possible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not make' to 'should make' weakens the prohibition. Replacing 'prior written consent' with 'first attempting to obtain the prior written consent' and adding 'if possible' introduces ambiguity about whether consent is actually required and under what circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Implied Duty of Good Faith (England and Wales)
perturbation.law_citation: Yam Seng Pte Ltd v International Trade Corp Ltd [2013] EWHC 111 (QB)
perturbation.law_explanation: English contract law imposes an implied duty of good faith, especially in relational contracts. While the NDA isn't necessarily a relational contract, the original clause aims to protect the parties' reputations and confidential information by requiring consent before public references. The modified clause introduces ambiguity by weakening the obligation and suggesting that consent may not always be necessary or obtainable. This could be interpreted as acting in bad faith, potentially undermining the purpose of the agreement and causing reputational damage, which the implied duty of good faith seeks to prevent. The case of Yam Seng Pte Ltd v International Trade Corp Ltd [2013] EWHC 111 (QB) highlights the growing recognition of an implied duty of good faith in English contract law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates that 'neither party shall make' any public reference without prior written consent. The changed text weakens this prohibition by stating that 'neither party should make' any reference, only requiring an attempt to obtain consent and adding 'if possible,' introducing ambiguity. This alteration undermines the original, clear obligation to obtain consent, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith, which the LLM's Law Explanation posits, particularly concerning protecting reputations and confidential information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a generic pointer to UK legislation and provides no specific contradicting laws, the perturbation explanation makes a case that the modified clause introduces ambiguity about whether consent is actually required. This ambiguity weakens the original obligation, supporting the assessment that a legal contradiction exists under 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition because it makes the enforceability difficult.
